title: An Essay Concerning Human Understanding — Book II, Chapter XXXIII: 'Of the Association of Ideas'
key_thesis: Locke coins the phrase 'association of ideas' and argues that ideas not naturally allied can be linked in the mind by chance, custom, or education, and that these acquired connections explain much of human reasoning, prejudice, and error.
relevance_to_combinatorial_creativity: Locke is the origin point: he first names 'association of ideas' as a discrete cognitive phenomenon. For an LLM ideation system, this is the philosophical license for treating concept-pairing itself as the basic mental operation. It also warns that arbitrary combinations form readily — implying the system must distinguish meaningful combinations from spurious ones.
memorable_quote: 'Some of our ideas have a natural correspondence and connexion with one another... Besides this there is another connexion of ideas wholly owing to chance or custom. Ideas, that in themselves are not at all of kin, come to be so united in some men's minds, that 'tis very hard to separate them.' (Essay II.xxxiii.5)

title: A Treatise of Human Nature — Book I, Part I, §IV: 'Of the Connexion or Association of Ideas'
key_thesis: Hume reduces the principles of mental association to three: resemblance, contiguity in time or place, and cause-and-effect. These are a 'gentle force' that uniformly determines how the imagination moves from one idea to another.
relevance_to_combinatorial_creativity: Hume gives the first taxonomy of association rules — the three principles are still the de facto vocabulary for retrieval-and-combination systems (similarity-based, context-based, causal). Any LLM ideation pipeline that pairs concepts implicitly chooses among these three operators; making the choice explicit grounds the system in a 300-year tradition.
memorable_quote: 'Were ideas entirely loose and unconnected, chance alone would join them... we are therefore to regard [association] as a gentle force, which commonly prevails.' (Treatise I.i.iv)

title: An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding — §III: 'Of the Association of Ideas'
key_thesis: A more accessible restatement of the three associative principles (resemblance, contiguity, causation), positioned as the universal laws by which thought moves — Hume's mature, public-facing version of his associationism.
relevance_to_combinatorial_creativity: The Enquiry is the canonical short-form citation for the three association principles and is more often cited than the Treatise in cognitive-science literature. Useful as the 'public' source for the associationist vocabulary the ideation system inherits.
memorable_quote: 'To me, there appear to be only three principles of connexion among ideas, namely, Resemblance, Contiguity in time or place, and Cause or Effect.'

title: Observations on Man, His Frame, His Duty, and His Expectations
key_thesis: Hartley fuses Locke's association of ideas with a physiological 'doctrine of vibrations,' arguing that simple ideas, repeatedly co-occurring, fuse into complex ideas via lawful neural processes — the first systematic mechanistic associationism.
relevance_to_combinatorial_creativity: Hartley turns association from a philosophical observation into a mechanism — the direct ancestor of connectionism, Hebbian learning, and embedding-space similarity. For an LLM combiner, Hartley is the historical analogue: repeated co-occurrence in a corpus produces fused vector representations.
memorable_quote: 'Any sensations A, B, C, etc., by being associated with one another a sufficient number of times, get such a power over the corresponding ideas a, b, c, etc., that any one of the sensations A, when impressed alone, shall be able to excite in the mind b, c, etc., the ideas of the rest.'

title: Critique of the Power of Judgment (Kritik der Urteilskraft) — esp. §§46–50 on genius and §49 on aesthetic ideas
venue_or_publisher: Lagarde und Friederich, Berlin and Libau
key_thesis: Kant distinguishes the merely reproductive imagination (which recombines what was perceived) from the productive imagination of genius, which generates 'aesthetic ideas' that bring the cognitive faculties into free play and exceed any concept that could be given.
relevance_to_combinatorial_creativity: Kant introduces the crucial distinction between mere recombination (reproductive) and genuine novel synthesis (productive). For an LLM ideation system this is the central design tension: most LLM 'creativity' is reproductive recombination — Kant clarifies what would have to be different for it to count as productive.
memorable_quote: 'Genius is the talent (natural endowment) which gives the rule to art... Genius is the innate mental disposition (ingenium) through which nature gives the rule to art.' (§46)

title: Biographia Literaria — esp. Chapter XIII: 'On the Imagination, or Esemplastic Power'
key_thesis: Coleridge sharply separates Fancy — a mechanical re-arrangement of fixed and dead memories — from Imagination, a vital, synthetic, 'esemplastic' (shaping-into-one) power that fuses opposites into a new organic unity.
relevance_to_combinatorial_creativity: Coleridge's Fancy/Imagination split is the literary-critical version of Kant's reproductive/productive distinction and the cleanest articulation of why mere combination is not enough. An LLM that only juxtaposes concepts is operating at the level of Fancy; Coleridge names what would make the leap to Imagination.
memorable_quote: 'FANCY... has no other counters to play with, but fixities and definites... it must receive all its materials ready made from the law of association.' (Ch. XIII)

title: Analysis of the Phenomena of the Human Mind
authors: James Mill (with notes by John Stuart Mill, Bain, Grote, Findlater in the 1869 ed.)
year: 1829 (1st ed.); 1869 (2nd ed. with J.S. Mill's notes)
venue_or_publisher: Baldwin and Cradock (1829); Longmans, Green, Reader & Dyer (1869)
key_thesis: James Mill pushes associationism to its strictest form: all mental life is built from sensations and ideas welded by association. J.S. Mill's notes amend this with the doctrine of 'mental chemistry' — that complex ideas can have emergent properties not reducible to their components.
relevance_to_combinatorial_creativity: J.S. Mill's mental-chemistry metaphor is the precise philosophical move that LLM combinatorial systems need: combinations of concepts can yield wholes whose meaning is not a linear sum of parts. This is the bridge from atomistic association to genuinely emergent ideation.
memorable_quote: 'The laws of the phenomena of mind are sometimes analogous to mechanical, but sometimes also to chemical laws... the simple ideas generate, rather than compose, the complex ones.' (J.S. Mill, A System of Logic VI.iv.3)

title: The Principles of Psychology — Vol. I, Chapter XIV: 'Association'
key_thesis: James reframes association: it is not ideas that associate but objects of thought, and the lawful unit is brain-process redintegration. He proposes one fundamental 'law of contiguity' (with similarity as a derivative case) and explains creative thought as guided by interest selecting among partial activations.
relevance_to_combinatorial_creativity: James updates Hume for the era of brain science and introduces the idea that selection (driven by interest) prunes the combinatorial explosion of associations — directly prefiguring the variation-and-selection accounts of Campbell and the goal-directed retrieval needed in LLM ideation.
memorable_quote: 'There is no other elementary causal law of association than the law of neural habit: When two elementary brain-processes have been active together or in immediate succession, one of them, on reoccurring, tends to propagate its excitement into the other.' (Vol. I, Ch. XIV)

title: Science et Méthode — Book I, Ch. III: 'L'invention mathématique' (English: 'Mathematical Creation', in Science and Method, trans. Maitland, 1914)
venue_or_publisher: Ernest Flammarion, Paris (French); Thomas Nelson & Sons, London (English)
key_thesis: Mathematical discovery proceeds in stages — conscious preparation, an unconscious incubation in which the mind combines ideas in vast numbers, an aesthetic 'sieve' that lets only the elegant combinations into consciousness, and finally verification.
relevance_to_combinatorial_creativity: Poincaré is the first explicit articulation of creativity as massive subconscious combinatorial search filtered by an aesthetic / utility criterion — exactly the architecture of an LLM ideation agent that generates many candidate combinations and ranks them. His 'sieve of beauty' is the prototype of the scoring function.
memorable_quote: 'Ideas rose in crowds; I felt them collide until pairs interlocked, so to speak, making a stable combination... Among chosen combinations the most fertile will often be those formed of elements drawn from domains which are far apart.'

title: The Art of Thought
venue_or_publisher: Jonathan Cape, London (and Harcourt, Brace, New York)
key_thesis: Wallas systematizes Poincaré's introspections into a four-stage model of the creative process: Preparation, Incubation, Illumination, and Verification (with an implicit fifth stage, Intimation, between Incubation and Illumination).
relevance_to_combinatorial_creativity: The four-stage model is the canonical workflow template for any creativity-generating system, including LLM agents: gather material, allow combinatorial mixing without conscious direction, surface the candidate, then test it. It maps cleanly to a multi-step agent pipeline.
memorable_quote: 'In the daily stream of thought these four different stages constantly overlap each other as we explore different problems... we are dealing with separable stages, and not separable mental events.' (Ch. IV)

title: Blind Variation and Selective Retention in Creative Thought as in Other Knowledge Processes
key_thesis: Creative thought is a Darwinian process: a generator produces variations 'blindly' (i.e., without foresight of which will succeed), and a selection mechanism retains the ones that fit problem constraints. Knowledge growth in general follows this BVSR pattern.
key_concepts: preservation and propagation of selected variants
relevance_to_combinatorial_creativity: BVSR is the most influential modern formal model of combinatorial creativity and underwrites the generate-and-test architecture of any LLM ideation system. 'Blind' here is technical — it means uncorrelated with the eventual solution criterion, not random — which sets a precise standard the system can be measured against.
memorable_quote: 'A blind-variation-and-selective-retention process is fundamental to all inductive achievements, to all genuine increases in knowledge, to all increases in fit of system to environment.'

title: The Associative Basis of the Creative Process
key_thesis: Creativity is the forming of associative elements into new combinations that meet specified requirements. Creative individuals have 'flat' associative hierarchies (many weak, far-flung associates to a stimulus) rather than 'steep' ones (a few dominant associates), enabling them to reach remote combinations.
key_concepts: serendipity / similarity / mediation as routes to combination
relevance_to_combinatorial_creativity: Mednick gives the operational definition of combinatorial creativity that is closest to what an LLM ideation system actually does: take semantically distant inputs and find the connecting concept. The RAT is also the standard benchmark for testing combinatorial creativity in humans and in models — directly usable as an eval.
memorable_quote: 'We may proceed to define the creative thinking process as the forming of associative elements into new combinations which either meet specified requirements or are in some way useful. The more mutually remote the elements of the new combination, the more creative the process or solution.'

title: The Act of Creation
key_thesis: Every creative act — humour, scientific discovery, artistic invention — is an act of 'bisociation': the sudden interlocking of two previously unrelated 'matrices of thought,' each a self-consistent rule-governed frame, perceived simultaneously in a single situation.
key_concepts: the Jester / the Sage / the Artist (humour, science, art as the three domains of bisociation)
relevance_to_combinatorial_creativity: Koestler is the single most important reference for an LLM ideation system that combines concepts. 'Bisociation' is precisely what the system attempts: cross-pollination between two domains/matrices that are normally kept separate. He also gives a concrete typology (the three domains) and worked examples (Gutenberg, Archimedes) that translate directly into prompt patterns.
memorable_quote: 'I have coined the term "bisociation" in order to make a distinction between the routine skills of thinking on a single "plane," as it were, and the creative act, which... always operates on more than one plane.' (Book One, Ch. II)

title: The Creative Mind: Myths and Mechanisms
key_thesis: Boden distinguishes three kinds of creativity — combinational (novel juxtaposition of familiar ideas), exploratory (search within a conceptual space), and transformational (changing the rules of the conceptual space itself) — and argues all three can in principle be modelled computationally.
relevance_to_combinatorial_creativity: Boden gives the modern computational vocabulary that connects the historical lineage above to AI systems. Her three-part typology lets the ideation project explicitly state which kind of creativity it targets (combinational), and her conceptual-space framework formalizes what a 'domain' or 'matrix' is in computational terms.
memorable_quote: 'Combinational creativity involves the unfamiliar combination of familiar ideas... It is exploratory and transformational creativity that pose the deepest puzzles.' (Ch. 3)
